problem: Implement a concurrent worker pool in Go. `NewPool(workers int) *Pool` creates a pool. `Submit(job func())` adds a job. `Shutdown()` waits for all jobs to complete and stops workers. No goroutine leaks allowed.
justification: Solution B correctly uses sync.WaitGroup to track goroutine lifecycle. Shutdown() calls wg.Wait() ensuring all jobs complete before returning — critical for graceful shutdown. Solution A's Shutdown() only closes the channel but never waits, meaning the caller has no guarantee that jobs have finished. A also leaks goroutines because there's no WaitGroup to signal completion. B uses defer wg.Done() which is idiomatic Go for cleanup. B also buffers the channel (workers*2) to reduce blocking on Submit().
ai_critique: Solution A has a critical goroutine leak: after Shutdown() returns, workers may still be running. Without sync.WaitGroup, the caller cannot know when all jobs are done. Solution B handles this correctly with wg.Add(1) before each goroutine and defer wg.Done() inside. B's buffered channel is also a practical improvement — it prevents Submit() from blocking when workers are briefly busy.
